title: Langues de chat
subtitle: Crisp, paper-thin French butter biscuits
excerpt: Very thin, crisp French biscuits piped from a simple butter batter and baked until the edges are golden. Light and delicate, they go with ice cream, mousse, and coffee in equal measure.
sourceNotes: Langues de chat (cat's tongues) are documented in French patisserie literature from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg, gives them as a standard accompaniment to desserts and ices. The name refers to the flat, oval shape said to resemble a cat's tongue. They are among the simplest French biscuits to make.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to four days. They are at their crispest on the day of baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-mixing technique in which butter and sugar are beaten together until pale and fluffy before eggs and flour are added.

content.text: Langues de chat are among the simplest biscuits in the French repertoire. The batter, made by the
with equal weights of butter, sugar, egg whites, and flour, pipes easily and bakes in under ten minutes. The result is a flat, pale biscuit with golden edges and a clean, sweet butter flavour.

They are used in French patisserie primarily as an accompaniment: alongside a sorbet, a mousse, or a cup of coffee. They are also the shell used in charlottes, pressed against the inside of a mould. At home, they are worth knowing as a simple, elegant biscuit that is ready in 30 minutes with pantry ingredients.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Beat {{unsalted-butter}} with {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} until pale. Add {{egg-whites}} a little at a time, mixing after each addition. Fold in sifted {{plain-flour}} until just combined. The batter should be smooth, thick, and pipe-able.

Fill a piping bag fitted with a 5 mm plain nozzle. Pipe 6 cm strips onto lined baking trays, spacing them well apart — they spread. Alternatively, drop teaspoons of batter and spread thin with the back of a damp spoon. Bake for 7–9 minutes until the edges are golden and the centres are pale. Do not overbake; the biscuits continue to crisp as they cool on the rack.
Soft butter (not melted) is essential: the batter must hold a piped shape without spreading too much before the oven sets it.
To use as charlotte shells: pipe longer strips (10–12 cm) and trim to fit the mould after baking.
The batter can be piped directly onto the tray without a nozzle; a small palette knife spreads the rounds thinner for an even crispier result.
